column37: Difficult questions that I've been unable to answer via my own research have *never* been answered. They are usually just ignored, and I don't have reputation to add a bounty.  Question 'difficulty' is not a thing that seems to be understood by the system.  Perhaps questions that receive very little attention, or have a lot of reads, and no answers, need priority/difficulty boosts?    The most common attempt to answer difficult problems is that people will try and rephrase my question for me, and/or they will tell me that I'm doing it wrong, and give me an unrelated answer about their opinion of what I should do instead. If I wanted opinions for alternative answers or approached, I would ask for that. I want an answer to the exact question that I asked. If there is no possible answer, or it is invalid, only then should they say so. Otherwise, the question should remain open, and 'difficulty' should be increased.    Another common issue with SO, is that answers are years out of date. There should be a mechanism for answers correct-ness to decay with time. People could indicate that an answer was helpful, indicating that the answer is still correct, or for old answers to be flagged as out-of-date and re-cycled for new up-to-date answers again.  Different question tags will have different 'frequencies'; questions about cobol or fortran are unlikely to decay, but questions about Rust or D, are likely to decay in correct-ness very quickly.    The single worst thing about SO is old answers propagating incorrect information, which are no longer active.
column38: SO is good to get answers to common problems. Uncommon problems occasionally yield decent answers. Hard problems are usually re-interpreted and answered un-helpfully, or get ignored because they're hard.    Old answers that are out-of-date are a serious problem. SO is the biggest source of propagating old/bad information because of SO's gravity. SO needs to work harder to combat this problem.

The thing I have learned during my time at SO is that reputation points should not be confused with skill or expertise. Reputation is a measure of how many people you have helped but does not include information about the problem you have helped solving. Since common problems are more likely to be faced my many developers they are also more likely to generate a decent amount of reputation. Highly specific problems and their solutions usually do not gain a similar visibility though. While I do not criticise the reputation system in general, I'd like to see a measure which actually tries to take problem and solution complexity and 'uniqueness' into account. The community does a decent job in identifying duplicates. However, there are just too many posts which a more or less the 20th attempt to read out an array in Javascript or the 5th copy of passages directly from the Python documentation about collections.

Stackoverflow became newbie unfriendly community. People often ask duplicate questions, or some simple ones that could be answered by a 1 min google search. There is much hate towards these people and the bar of acceptable question should be lowered a bit. There are people who english is not their primary language and they can't find the right keywords to search for a problem so they post on stackoverflow with a description of a problem. You can at least treat them with some respect, they are not thugs or scum of the community.    Another problem is comments from people with a lot of points. For example, I had a js code that polled some data every 2 seconds using setInterval, sometimes the task took longer than 2 and the api calls that I made where kind of becoming like a ddos attack to the servers. So I asked the question of how to execute a command in intervals of X seconds but wait if the command takes longer than X seconds. I had to use a tail-recursive setTimeout algorithm to solve this problem which I was unaware of how to implement at the time. Then this guy with the points tells me what and why you want to do this, just use a bigger interval to avoid the overlap. When I ask for a question, I am waiting for an actual  solution, not a fix. I told him that this doesn't answer my question and the conditions for it are very specific. I didn't want to give much context, because I fking can't. And because he has a ton of points more than me, his words are sacred and I now defiled a god, so I got bombarded by downvotes. I deleted that post, and I deleted some other questions like these, because people go for points and they don't want to actually answer the questions, just to post a quick fix that will work for a short period of time and get some quick upvotes.    I am trying to keep a distance from this toxic community and remain an anonymous copy paster.

Existing good answers often get voted down in order to make a new answer more visible, even if it is no better.    People with higher reputation tend to get voted up, as if simply having reputation gains you more reputation, out of some illusion of higher expertise rather than recognition of the fact that their only expertise is in how to game the system.    Good solutions often end up in comments rather than answers, simply because the way the two are differentiated makes the comment the more appropriate place to look, in spite of the fact that people coming to the site in search of answers will be quickly scanning the answers of multiple search results, rather than wasting their work day carefully reading each comment on a page.

Moderator is strong point.

I have over 30 years of Unix and Linux software development and contribute my expertise widely within the GitHub and other open source community forms and meet ups.  However, the first several attempts to participate on Stack Overflow resulted in very negative interactions from moderators marking my problem question as redundant with no obvious mechanism to debate that fact.  In one case I remember the only way it could be justifiable flagged as duplicate was that the solution was exposed in a different package that I wasnØ¹t using.  The root cause was a duplicate, but how it was exposed to different developers using different software libraries involved different stack traces and error messages.  You therefore couldnØ¹t find the answer without KNOWING the answer to the problem.    It appears your moderators are incentivized to just take down posts to the detriment of your site. The result is in my opinion is a Ø¹gotchaØ¹ culture on Stack Overflow that I donØ¹t really have the bandwidth or incentive to interact with. Therefore, I just mine you indirectly from Google and no longer trouble myself to try participate in Stack Overflow even when I know I have the answer or a better answer than exists on your site.

column37: Voting up answers is one of the most useful parts of stack overflow. Before it's approved, an upvoted answer can act as a consensus approval even when the question asker is inactive. After it's answered, the upvote can be seen as a "this still works". Furthermore, by upvoting, you prevent duplicate answers. However, you need 15 reputation to upvote at all, which puts limits on those effects.
Sometimes I see lots of similar answers to a certain question, because people want to gain reputation, no matter what.

The site needs some kind of penalty for users who do not participate anymore. People who participated in the beginning, when there was less content competing for popularity, are disproportionally compensated even if they do not engage anymore. This discourages new users from participating.
